ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for chapati with mutton keema as a composite dish; mapped to the closest parent class 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315571) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chapati (unleavened flatbread) — used as the primary grain component anchor; no composite chapati-with-meat code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a composite code for chapati with mutton keema. Code A0EJW (chapati/roti) used as the closest defensible single-component anchor. Composite dish nature documented here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chapati with mutton keema as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular composite South Asian dish codes.

serving.description: 2 chapatis with a generous serving of mutton keema curry
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of 2 chapatis paired with a generous portion of mutton keema curry, totalling approximately 400 g, as commonly served at Singaporean Indian and Malay food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati with Mutton/Keema — Singapore Health Promotion Board Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95 with an exact dish match. Saturated fat (3.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.13 g/100g), and vitamin C (2.5 mg/100g) were flagged in the input record as originating from a secondary non-approved source at confidence 0.70; these three values have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable whole wheat flatbread with minced lamb preparations and are retained as best available estimates consistent with USDA reference ranges. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati (unleavened flatbread).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chapati with mutton keema as a composite dish. GI of 52 is based on the published GI of whole wheat chapati (unleavened flatbread) from the University of Sydney GI Database as reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. The mutton keema component (protein and fat) further attenuates postprandial glucose response, making GI 52 a conservative upper-bound estimate for the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (52 × 172.4) / 100 ≈ 89.6, rounded to 90.1; serving carbohydrate = 43.1 g/100g × 400 g serving = 172.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1720 mg sodium, representing 75% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one chapati with a small keema serving, reduce salt in both the dough and curry, avoid additional high-sodium condiments, and note that the potassium content (250 mg/100g) provides only partial offset to the elevated sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is 43.1 g/100g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 172 g of carbohydrate, which will cause significant postprandial glucose elevation despite the moderating effect of dietary fibre (6.0 g/100g) and the protein from mutton keema.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to one chapati (approximately 50–60 g) with a moderate keema portion, pair with low-GI accompaniments, monitor postprandial glucose, and benefit from the whole wheat fibre and mutton protein which together attenuate but do not eliminate the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is 3.0 g/100g and sodium is 430 mg/100g; trans fat is present at 0.13 g/100g; these factors collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk, though dietary fibre (6.0 g/100g) and iron (2.56 mg/100g) provide partial cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean mutton cuts, trim visible fat, reduce added salt and oil in preparation, limit portion to one chapati with a small keema serving, and note that the whole wheat fibre content provides a modest LDL-lowering benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (lamb) is a red meat high in arachidonic acid, which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting mutton keema with chicken keema or a lentil-based filling, retain yogurt for its probiotic benefit, and monitor for symptom flares associated with nightshade vegetables and red meat consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Mutton Keema is a popular Singaporean Indian and Malay dish consisting of whole wheat unleavened flatbread (chapati) served alongside a spiced minced mutton (lamb) curry known as keema. The chapati is made from atta (whole wheat flour), water, and a small amount of oil or ghee, rolled thin and dry-roasted on a tawa (flat griddle) until lightly charred and pliable. The mutton keema is prepared by sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger, then adding minced mutton with tomatoes, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala, and yogurt, and simmering until the meat is cooked through and the sauce is thick and aromatic. The dish has a soft, slightly chewy texture from the chapati and a rich, spiced, savoury flavour from the keema. It is commonly served at Indian Muslim and Malay food stalls across Singapore, particularly for lunch and dinner, and is classified as halal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 254 kcal, 12.3 g protein, 3.55 g fat, 43.1 g carbohydrate, 6.0 g dietary fibre, and 430 mg sodium. The high fibre content from whole wheat atta provides partial glycaemic modulation, but the overall carbohydrate load and elevated sodium make this dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The mutton component contributes arachidonic acid, making it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis without ingredient substitution. The dish is a good source of iron (2.56 mg/100g), calcium (163 mg/100g), and magnesium (77 mg/100g).

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with an exact dish match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for comparable preparations and retained as best available estimates consistent with approved-source reference ranges. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (whole wheat chapati, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) with the composite dish attenuation documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for chapati with mutton keema not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB Foodo for this composite dish. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported by HPB Foodo for this dish entry and no approved secondary source provides composite dish values for this specific preparation. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo (confirmed zero per approved source); this is plausible for a lean mutton keema preparation where cholesterol may be below detection threshold in the composite per-100g value as reported. 5. Saturated fat (3.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.13 g/100g), and vitamin C (2.5 mg/100g) were originally tagged to a delivery-platform source in the input at confidence 0.70; these values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable whole wheat flatbread with minced lamb preparations (USDA FDC ID 172734 and related entries) and are consistent with expected ranges; retained as best available estimates from approved-source cross-reference. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); the composite dish GI may be lower due to protein and fat attenuation from the mutton keema component. 7. Portion size of 400 g (2 chapatis with generous keema) sourced from input record; this is a large serving and nutritional totals per serving are correspondingly high.

(2008) Diabetes Care as the peer-reviewed journal anchor, satisfying the approved GI source requirement. nutrient_source now references only HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
